title: Om Ali
subtitle: Egyptian bread pudding with cream, nuts, and raisins
excerpt: Torn croissants or puff pastry baked with warm milk, cream, sugar, almonds, raisins, and coconut until the pastry soaks up the cream and the top turns golden. Egypt's most celebrated dessert. Closer to a bread and butter pudding than to the Moroccan pastilla, despite the shared ingredients.
sourceNotes: Om Ali (literally 'Mother of Ali') is the Egyptian bread and cream pudding, said to date from the Ayyubid period and associated with the wife of the Sultan Baybars. Traditionally made with thin layers of pastry (feteer or puff pastry), it is also commonly made with croissants in modern Cairo. It is served hot and is Egypt's most recognised dessert internationally.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 2 hours ahead and bake when needed.

content.text: Day-old croissants or a baked sheet of puff pastry work equally well. Day-old croissants have the right dry, slightly stale texture to absorb the cream without becoming completely sodden. Fresh croissants are too moist and produce a gluey result.

The cream and milk mixture is poured hot over the pastry pieces and then the whole thing is baked. The top should be golden and bubbling when it comes out of the oven. It must be eaten hot — it does not reheat well and collapses as it cools.

items.prepNote: day-old, torn into pieces, or use 320 g baked puff pastry

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C. Tear the {{croissant}} into rough 3 cm pieces and spread them in a large, deep baking dish. Scatter the {{blanched-almonds}}, {{raisins}}, and {{desiccated-coconut}} over and around the pastry. Heat the {{whole-milk}}, {{double-cream}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a saucepan over medium heat until steaming and the sugar has dissolved — do not boil. Pour the hot cream mixture evenly over the pastry and nuts. The pastry should be submerged. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the top is golden and the cream is bubbling around the edges. Serve immediately, spooned into bowls.

items.cause: Croissants too fresh or too much liquid
items.fix: Use day-old or oven-dried croissants. If using fresh, tear and dry in a low oven (150°C) for 10 minutes first. The pastry should absorb the cream without dissolving

items.cause: Oven temperature too low or cream covering the top
items.fix: Ensure the pastry pieces protrude above the cream level before baking. If still pale at 20 minutes, switch to grill for 2 to 3 minutes, watching closely

items.cause: Croissants already sweet plus added sugar
items.fix: Reduce sugar to 2 tablespoons with buttery croissants. Taste the cream mixture before pouring and adjust

content.text: Om Ali is the Egyptian national dessert. It appears on the menu of every Egyptian restaurant, is made at home for celebrations and family gatherings, and is sold from sweet shops throughout the country. The legend attached to it (that it was made by the victorious wife of the Sultan to celebrate the defeat of her rival) gives it a narrative weight unusual for a pudding. In Cairo's five-star hotels, om ali is made from feteer meshaltet (Egyptian flaky pastry), which gives a lighter result than croissants. Both versions are served piping hot, which is non-negotiable.
